name: Dr Christopher Edwards
school: Department of Astronomy and Planetary Science
focus: Infrared remote sensing • Planetary surfaces • Mars and lunar missions
bio: Christopher S. Edwards is an Associate Professor of Planetary Science at Northern Arizona University whose work spans laboratory spectral analysis, planetary remote sensing data analysis, numerical modeling, field analog studies, and instrument development for Earth and planetary science. He serves as a science team member and instrument scientist for the Emirates Mars Infrared Spectrometer on the Emirates Mars Mission Hope, is a participating scientist on the Mars Science Laboratory Curiosity Rover and the OSIRIS-REx mission to Bennu, and is a Co-Investigator on the Lunar Trailblazer mission. His research focuses on the composition, physical properties, and processes of planetary surfaces, especially rocky bodies such as Earth and Mars, using remote sensing techniques that connect rover, orbital, laboratory, and field datasets. Edwards also leads and contributes to instrument development across space, laboratory, and field settings, including work such as thermophysics studies from rover to orbit, the VISIONS instrument headed to Mars aboard NASA's ESCAPADE mission, and UAV-based remote sensing of sites like the Iron King Mine to study Earth analog and Earth system processes. Across these efforts, he integrates spectroscopy, radiometry, field observations, and modeling to better interpret planetary and terrestrial surfaces, and he has been involved in six missions either in operation or development, serving as instrument lead on three.
